coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical exfoliation of phosphorus doped carbon dots with dual-emission originating from carbon core and surface states
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Xuehua
Li, Yuan
Lei, Guoxuan
Ding, Hui
Wang, Li
Li, Xiaobing
Zhao, Hui
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots have attracted significant attention due to their versatile and excellent photoelectric properties; however, the origins and underlying mechanism remain a subject of debate especially for dual-emission CDs. Here, novel dual-emission CDs have been exfoliated from graphite rods via an electrochemical method in a dihydrogen phosphate electrolyte. The CDs possess a very narrow blue emission band in the short wavelength region and a broad cyan emission band in the long wavelength range, which are attributed to carbon core state and surface state from phosphorus atom doping, respectively. Interestingly, the former shows excitation-dependent behavior, but the latter is excitation-independent. Thus, as the excitation wavelength increases, the blue emission band is gradually closing to the cyan one and coalesce into a broad peak at the excitation wavelength of 420 nm. The variations of both emission intuitively illustrate the role transition between a core state and the doped surface state at different excitation wavelengths, which offer a real sample for the study of transition between emissive states. Our findings provide novel and fundamental insights on the fluorescence mechanism of CDs with dual-emission.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Dual-emission
Electrochemical exfoliation
Transition of emissive state

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image and (b) corresponding size distribution of CDs (inset).
(a) FTIR spectrum of CDs, (b)XPS survey spectrum, (c), (d) and (e) the high-resolution XPS spectra of P 2p, C 1s, and O1s, respectively.
UV–visible absorption spectrum (left) and fluorescence emission spectrum (right) under excitation of 320 nm.
3D waterfall fluorescence spectra of CDs excited at increasing wavelengths.

head.ce:title: Electrochemical exfoliation of phosphorus doped carbon dots with dual-emission originating from carbon core and surface states

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Engineering Research Center of Coal Preparation and Purification
China University of Mining and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots have attracted significant attention due to their versatile and excellent photoelectric properties; however, the origins and underlying mechanism remain a subject of debate especially for dual-emission CDs. Here, novel dual-emission CDs have been exfoliated from graphite rods via an electrochemical method in a dihydrogen phosphate electrolyte. The CDs possess a very narrow blue emission band in the short wavelength region and a broad cyan emission band in the long wavelength range, which are attributed to carbon core state and surface state from phosphorus atom doping, respectively. Interestingly, the former shows excitation-dependent behavior, but the latter is excitation-independent. Thus, as the excitation wavelength increases, the blue emission band is gradually closing to the cyan one and coalesce into a broad peak at the excitation wavelength of 420 nm. The variations of both emission intuitively illustrate the role transition between a core state and the doped surface state at different excitation wavelengths, which offer a real sample for the study of transition between emissive states. Our findings provide novel and fundamental insights on the fluorescence mechanism of CDs with dual-emission.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as an emerging member of carbon-based materials family, usually consist of a core of sp2/sp3 hybridized carbon atoms and various surface groups, endowing it with excellent optical/electronic properties, high solubility, low toxicity and greater chemical stability [1–3]. These remarkable merits arouse extensive research interests and make CDs promising for application in many fields such as sensing, bio-imaging, catalytic, optoelectronics [4–7] and so on. Current preparation methods can be classified into breaking down large carbonaceous materials (top-down approaches) or carbonizing various organic matter or small carbon-containing molecules (bottom-up approaches) [8,9]. Hence, the formation of carbon core and surface groups of CDs is heavily dependent on prepared routes, nature of the precursors and reaction conditions used [10–12]. These structures further determine fluorescence behaviors and intrinsic mechanisms of CDs, and thus a great amount of effort has been devoted to exploring this structure-activity relationship. However, a significant knowledge gap still persists in our fundamental understanding of how CDs actually fluoresce.

Up to now, the popularly accepted fluorescence mechanisms of CDs mainly refers to the carbon core state and surface state and synergistic effect between them [10,13–15]. The carbon core state is known as the intrinsic state caused by the quantum confinement effect or size effect and usually accounts for short wavelength emission, which is an extensively applicable fluorescence mechanism especially for CDs with lattice structure or a high degree of graphitization [16]. The core “size” refers to the sp2 domain-the effective conjugation length-rather than the actual particle size. The surface state is usually considered to control energy levels of CDs by hybridization of the carbon backbone with heteroatoms or by connecting surface groups, generally resulting in long wavelength emission [17]. Therefore, the hybrid approach is also applied to adjust the fluorescence of CDs by altering types of surface groups. At present, most of CDs usually display a single fluorescent emission band, the mechanism of which can be always assigned to core state or surface state. However, for CDs with dual or multiple emission bands at the same excitation wavelength, a single mechanism fails to fully explain all fluorescence behavior. Furthermore, the trend of every emission band at different excitation wavelengths directly indicates the effect variations of core state and surface state in the synergistic process. From another perspective, dual-emission or multiple-emission can intuitively illustrate the transitions and relationships of the core and surface state, which is instrumental in understanding fluorescent behavior of CDs.

Besides, dual-emission CDs shows great application potential in the field ratiometric probes, because two emission bands can effectively avoid instrumental fluctuations and background interferences through the built-in correction of the reference signal and significantly improve the sensitivity and accuracy of detection [18]. Many ratiometric fluorescent probes based on CDs were prepared by co-dopin [10,19], carbonizing two materials [20,21] and covalently binding CDs with other specific fluorescent agents [22,23]. Although bottom-up routes are easy to obtain dual-emission, the comprehensive polymerizing-carbonizing process significantly enhance the complexity of fluorescence analysis. Hence, a top-down method for the preparation of dual-emission CDs may be conducive to elucidate the fluorescence mechanism. In addition, there are rarely reported on dual-emission CDs by top-down methods. Because the CDs cutting down carbonaceous materials usually inherit stable graphite structure, they are difficult to be attached by functional groups due to lack of bond condition in the top-down process. Therefore, the second emission center is unable to be achieved owing to either the lack of heteroatoms doped into carbon backbone or no functional groups attached on the surface of CDs.

Herein, a novel dual-emission CDs is firstly electrochemically exfoliated from graphite rods in the electrolyte of sodium dihydrogen phosphate. The obtained CDs show a narrow blue emission band with a sharp profile (small full width at half maximum (FWHM) about 15 nm) in the short wavelength region and a broad cyan emission band exists in the long wavelength region. The structural and surface chemical characterizations are indicative of successful phosphorus atom doping into the carbon skeleton, which acting as the surface state for the second emissive center. Interestingly, as the excitation wavelength increase, the blue emission was gradually approaching to the other band until finally both band coalesce into a broad band at last, due to different dependences on excitation wavelength. The distinct fluorescent behavior is believed to originate from different emissive states, namely carbon core state and surface state. Furthermore, fluorescence variation with increasing excitation intuitively illustrates the role transition of core state and surface state in synergistic process. Hence, our finding have a great significance for the investigation of the relationship between both states and further understanding of the fundamental fluorescence mechanism.

Both graphite rods with 10 cm length and 0.6 cm diameter were rinsed sequentially in ethanol and in distilled water for half an hour by ultrasonic equipment. In classical two-electrode configuration, they were vertically inserted in electrolyte with a distance of 2 cm, which were adopted as working electrode and the counter electrode, respectively. Sodium dihydrogen phosphate 1.2 g was dissolved in distilled water (100 mL) as electrolyte. After applying a potential of 15 V by a direct current power on both rods, a great number of bubbles released on the surface of electrodes due to the dissociation of water. As the reaction proceeds, the colorless electrolyte gradually changed to yellow, to brown and finally to dark brown, indicating that the CDs were exfoliated from graphite rods and existed in this dark brown solution. In order to accelerate the reaction, a magnetic stirrer continued to stir the electrolyte during the whole experiment. The solution was firstly centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 20 min to remove bigger graphite particles and then was filtered with slow-speed quantitative filter paper. Finally, the obtained solution contained a great number of CDs, which can be further freeze dried to obtain solid CDs.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of CDs were characterized by a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (Jeol, Japan) at an accelerating voltage of 200 KV. The Nano Measurer (verion 1.2) was used to count the size distribution of CDs on the basis of TEM images (counting more than 200 dots for each sample in different images). Light absorption behavior was measured using ultraviolet–visible (Uv–vis) spectrophotometer (UV-3600, Shimadzu, Japan). The Fourier-Transform infrared spectra were obtained using a Nicolet 5700 FTIR spectrophotometer (Nicolet, USA) in 400–4000 cm−1. The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) of CDs were recorded on a PHI 5000 Versa Probe electron energy spectrometer (UIVAC-PHI, Japan). The NMR experiments were performed at room temperature in a Bruker AVANCE-III HD 600 spectrometer equipped with MAS probe. The measurement of fluorescence spectra was carried out on a Fluoromax-4 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Horiba, Japan). The quantum yield of CDs was achieved by using a comparative method [24].

The morphological features of the as-exfoliated CDs were characterized by TEM technique, and the taken images and their corresponding size distribution histograms are shown in Fig. 1 (a) and (b), respectively. It can be easily observed that most of the CDs show a quasi-spherical shape and are well monodisperse in aqueous solution without any aggregation, indicating a good dispersion. After statistically counting more than 200 dots from several images, the size distribution histogram was obtained by the software of Nano Measurer 1.2. As shown in Fig. 1 (b), the CDs have dimensions of 0.5–4.5 nm and mainly distributed in range of 1.5–2.5 nm, and a fitting Gaussian curve reveals the average size of the CDs is 2.05 nm, which was smaller than most previous reports due to the weak corrosion ability of the electrolyte [25]. Although many efforts have been devoted to obtain the crystal information through the high resolution TEM (HRTEM), unfortunately, no image clear enough to observe the lattice fringe was taken owing to the small size of the CDs. Only some indistinct crystal lattices are observed in the HRTEM images, as shown in Fig. S1 in Supplementary Material. These results reveal that the CDs have been successfully exfoliated down from the graphite rod, while the weak corrosion ability of sodium dihydrogen phosphate resulted in a smaller size of the CDs, comparing with the ones exfoliated in nitrates and sulfate solution [26].

FITR and XPS techniques were used to characterize the chemical composition and surface groups, which have a significant influence on the fluoresence behavior of the as-exfoliated CDs. As illustrated in Fig. 2 (a), the FTIR spectrum shows three distinct peaks, in which the broad peak at 3452 cm−1 and the slim peak at 1640 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibrations of –OH groups [27] and CO stretching vibrations on the surface of CDs [28,29], respectively. The sharp peak around 1049 cm−1 is attributed to P–O–C band. These absorption peaks not only suggest the successful doping of the phosphorus atom into the CDs, but also reveal the attachment of groups on the surface of CDs. Furthermore, XPS spectrum was recorded to further confirm the chemical state of surface groups on the CDs. As shown in Fig. 2 (b), the XPS survey spectrum shows four signals at 133.2, 283.0, 497.3 and 529.4 eV, corresponding to P 2p, C 1s, Na 1s and O 1s, respectively. In addition, two predominate peaks are C 1s and O 1s, indicating that the CDs mainly comprise elements of carbon and oxygen. The abundant carbon element originates from the carbon core exfoliated from graphite rods and the high oxygen content is attributed to the attachment of the oxygen-containing functional groups during the exfoliation process, which are demonstrated by the high-resolution spectra. In the spectrum of C 1s in Fig. 2(c), it can be deconvoluted into four different peaks of 284.7, 285.9, 286.3 and 288.2 eV, which are assigned to the CC, C–P, C–O and CO groups [22], respectively. The O 1s spectrum in Fig. 2(d) shows the presence of –OH groups at 530.5 eV, PO at 531.4 eV, C–O at 532.4 eV, and CO at 533.4 eV [30,31]. Besides, in the high-resolution spectrum of P 2p of Fig. 2(e), two peaks appear at 133.1 and 133.9 eV, corresponding to P–O and P–C bonds, respectively [7,22,32]. In order to further investigate phosphorus state, the solid-state 31P NMR spectrum of the CDs was recorded and the results are shown in Fig. S2 (in Supplementary Material). The dominant signal of the spectrum at −1.39 ppm and other weak signals around 114, 74, 49, −22 and −65 ppm are ascribed to dihydrogen phosphates-like structures [33,34]. It is should be noted that the signal at 12.34 (located in the range of 7–14 ppm) is most likely due to the compounds with C–P bonding [35], which provide another evidence that phosphorus atoms have been doped into the skeleton of carbon core. The presence of P–O bond may be attributed to the covalently bonded P–O–C group attached on the surface of CDs, and the P–C bond originated from some reaction between carbon and phosphorus atoms during the electrochemical exfoliation process, both of which are great agreement with FTIR results. These abundant surface groups or doping atoms would work with carbon core to endow the as-exfoliated CDs distinctive fluorescence behaviors.

The optical behavior of as-exfoliated CDs are investigated by UV–vis absorption and fluorescence techniques. As shown in Fig. 3 (left), the UV–vis spectrum of the CDs display a distinct peak centered at 236 nm, which is considered to be the typical π-π* transition characteristic peak of the sp2 domain in CC [36]. Furthermore, a weak shoulder peak appears around 370 nm which is possibly originated from the n-π* transition of CO groups on the surface of the CDs [37]. As the most significant behavior, the fluorescence spectrum of the CDs has been measured and shown in Fig. 3 (right). Under excitation of 320 nm, the CDs present two discrete fluorescence peaks at short wavelength of 363 nm (in the blue light region) and at long wavelength of 483 nm (locating around the boundary of cyan light), respectively. The most significant difference between the peaks is that the former has a sharp profile with a narrow FWHM about 15 nm, while the latter have a broad FWHM of 100 nm. The distinctive fluorescence behaviors of CDs suggest that although there is only one kind of CDs, there are referring to two different fluorescence mechanisms. Besides, the CDs have an excellent cyan emission under the light of 395 nm, as shown in the inset image of Fig. 3, and its quantum yield was calculated to be 8.3 % using quinine sulfate as reference (quantum yield in the 0.05 M sulfuric acid). The fluorescent excitation (FLE) spectrum of the CDs have been measured and shown in Fig. S4 (Supplementary Material). The FLE spectrum shows good symmetry, and the Stokes shifts of both blue and cyan emissions are 60 nm and 200 nm, respectively. Besides, the CDs show a great stability at temperature from 10 to 70 °C and in pH value of 3∼9 (as shown in Fig. S5 of Supplementary Material), which are ascribed to the stable core-shell structure of the CDs.

At present, due to the variety of CDs prepared by different methods from diverse precursors, the fluorescence mechanism of CDs is still an open debate. Moreover, a better understanding of the fluorescent behavior is needed to achieve controllable structure and adjustable fluorescence [13,38]. Nevertheless, through continuous efforts, the core-shell structure is considered to be explained for the fluorescence of the CD prepared by top-down methods and some down-up approaches. Herein, the CDs exfoliated in the electrolyte of dihydrogen phosphate show a blue emission peak and a cyan emission peak, which may be also attributed to a core-shell structure formed in the exfoliation process. The carbon core is the hybrid sp2/sp3 graphite nanoparticles from graphite rod by exfoliation, while the shell part includes the formed surface groups and the doped phosphorus atom in the carbon skeleton. The obtained CDs inherited a lattice structure from the graphite rod, mainly containing sp2 hybridization of CC bands and forming many sp2 subdomains [10]. Besides, during the exfoliation process, some oxygen radicals originating from water dissociation reacted with carbon atom and formed carboxyl functional groups (CO) with sp3 hybridization and –OH groups on the surface of as-prepared CDs, which can be also supported by the XPS and FTIR results. Above analysis of carbon core and surface groups is indicative that the exfoliated CDs have a hybridization structure of sp2 and sp3, in which the sp2 subdomain isolated by sp3 matrix. The π and π* electronic levels of sp2 subdomain lie within the bandgap of σ and σ* state of sp3 matrix, leading to a strong delocalization effect [16,39]. Thus, the sp2 subdomain isolated by sp3 matrix in the exfoliated CDs predominated the carbon core state, which are responsible for the blue sharp emission with narrow FWHM at short wavelength. In addition, it is worth noting that that a widely accepted work points out that the –OH group predominates the blue emission through a surface state. Thus, the blue emission of the exfoliated CDs may stem from the synergic effect of the carbon core and the abundant –OH groups on the surface.

Since the carbon core is responsible for the blue emission, the cyan emission should be ascribed to the surface groups or the doping of the phosphorus atom owing the deficiency of the material basis of other mechanisms. As verified by the FTIR and XPS results, the phosphorus atoms have been incorporated into the CDs during the electrochemical exfoliation, in which the electrolyte dihydrogen phosphate acts as a doping reagent. The as-exfoliated CDs is actually a kind of phosphorus-doped CDs (P-doped CDs). In fact, the sharp profile of the blue emission of the CDs is highly consistent with our previous one [29], in which the CDs was exfoliated under the same conditions except for using distilled water instead of dihydrogen phosphate solution. As illustrated in Fig. S3 of Supplementary Material, the comparison of fluorescence and surface groups is indicative that both CDs coincide exactly in the position and the shape of the blue emission peak, suggesting the identical fluorescence mechanism of the carbon core state. However, the CDs exfoliated in the dihydrogen phosphate solution significantly has one more cyan emission peak with broad FWHM. Comparing both FTIR spectra shown in Fig. S3 (b) (Supplementary Material), it is easily found that the CDs exfoliated in dihydrogen phosphate have one more P–O–C band around 1049 cm−1. The XPS spectra of the P-doped CDs also demonstrate the exisitence of C–P and P–O bands, indicating that phosphorus has been incorporated into the carbon skeleton. In addition, some previous reports have pointed out phosphorus atom doping can introduce additional energy levels, effectively creating more electron transition pathways in the band structure of CDs, which resulted in the cyan emission of theP-doped CDs [40–42]. Thus, it is reasonable to deduce that the doping phosphorus atom causes the cyan emission of the P-doped CDs in the fluorescence spectrum. Based on the above anlysis, the core-shell structure is responsible for the dual-emission of the exfoliated CDs in a solution of dihydrogen phosphate.

Subsequently, the fluorescence behavior of the P-doped CDs were investigated by increasing excitation wavelength from 320 to 420 nm with increment of 20 nm. As shown in the 3D waterfall fluorescence spectra of Fig. 4 , both emission peaks gradually decreased in intensity, but they showed different dependence on excitation wavelength. For the blue emission, when the excitation wavelength increased from 320 to 400 nm, its peak position shifted from 362 to 466 nm and the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased until it disappeared at the excitation wavelength of 420 nm. However, the cyan emission peaks always appears at around 488 nm under different excitation wavelengths and its intensity continuously decreased. In summary, the blue emission is dependent on excitation wavelength, while the cyan emission is independent. We have proposed that the blue emission stems from the synergy of carbon core and –OH groups in the above discussion, but this conclusion contradicts the phenomenon of dependency on excitation wavelength. If the –OH groups dominate the blue emission of the P-doped CDs, they will remain stable with increasing excitation wavelength and will emit blue fluorescence with a specific wavelength all the time, namely independence on excitation wavelength. Hence, the blue emission is predominated by the carbon core state, and the excitation-dependent behavior of fluorescence is assigned to the changing size of carbon core (namely, sp2 subdomain isolated by sp3 matrix). On the contrary, the excitation-independent phenomenon of cyan emission is ascribed to no change of the doped phosphorus atom in carbon skeleton during changing excitation wavelength. Another notable phenomenon is the intensity of the blue emission gradually decreasing with rising excitation wavelength and final disappearance under excitation at 420 nm. This phenomenon is attributed to the reduction of energy with the increase of excitation wavelength is not enough to excite the transition of photoelectron in carbon core. Meanwhile, the intensity of cyan emission was also decreasing caused by the same reduction of energy. However, these energies of excitation wavelength are still higher than the energy gap of cyan transition, and thus the intensity of cyan emission decreased but did not disappear.

Although the origin of fluorescence was proposed, the mechanism of the narrow FWHM of the blue emission has not been clarified. Inorganic quantum dots (QDs) are famous with the pure color fluorescence (with narrow FWHM), which is similar to CDs in fluorescent emission and morphology (small nanoparticles). It has been demonstrated that the electron-phonon coupling resulting from structural vibrations and distortions in the structure of QDs plays a dominating role in determining the FWHM of fluorescent peak [43]. Generally, strong electron-phonon coupling that arises from large structural distortion and vibration causes a broad emission spectrum of fluorophores since the interactions between electrons and various vibration modes form many bound states that have lower energy than a non-interacting system. Yuan et al. generalized relevant theory of the mechanism of triangle carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with the narrow fluorescent peaks [43–45]. They proposed that the high structural stability of triangular CQDs dramatically reduces electron-phonon coupling and the pure electron-donating hydroxyl groups at the sites of the CQDs increase the π electron cloud density and facilitate the pure radiative recombination of confined electrons and holes. The electrochemically exfoliated CDs is well consisted with the triangular CQDs in the stable structure. The dense structure and high stability of graphite rods were inherited and retained by as-exfoliated CDs, both which can weaken electron-phonon coupling. In addition, the functional groups, such as carboxyl and hydroxyl, can only exist on the edge or surface of CDs, because it is impossible for various groups to cross the surface and react with inside carbon atoms during the exfoliation process. Hence, the exfoliated CDs from graphite rods possess blue color fluorescence with narrow FWHM.

In this work, a P-doped CDs with dual-emission have been successfully exfoliated from graphite rods by electrochemical method in electrolyte of dihydrogen phosphate. The as-exfoliated CDs possess small size and simple surface groups and phosphorus atoms have been doped into the carbon skeleton, which is verified by characterizations of surface groups. The CDs display a unique dual-emission behavior, in which a very narrow blue emission appears in the short wavelength region and a broad cyan emission band exists in the long wavelength range. Interestingly, the blue emission shows excitation-dependent behavior, while the cyan peak shows independent-wavelength. Besides, as the excitation wavelength increases, the blue emission peaks are gradually closing to the latter and finally disappear in the spectrum at the excitation wavelength of 420 nm. The fluorescence mechanism suggests that the emissions are attributed to the carbon core state and surface state, respectively. The former stems from the sp2 subdomain isolated by sp3 matrix, while the latter originates from the surface state resulting from the doping of phosphorus atom into the carbon skeleton. The variation of both fluorescence emission bands illustrates the role transition between a core state and surface state at different excitation wavelengths, which have a great significance for the investigation of the relationship between both states in synergistic process. Our results promote a better understanding of the fluorescence mechanism of CDs with dual emissions.
All authors declare that there are no competing interests.

Xuehua Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Yuan Li: Resources, Data curation, Visualization. Guoxuan Lei: Data curation, Visualization. Hui Ding: Validation, Investigation. Li Wang: Formal analysis, Validation. Xiaobing Li: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Hui Zhao: Validation, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.52004279, 51803233), Natural Science Foundation of Henan Province (No.222300420500) and Key Technologies R & D Program of Henan Province (No. 212102310507).
